name: Sobrarbe Geopark BTT Route 5: Aínsa to El Pueyo Loop
name_original: Badlands del Soto y Pueyo de Araguás — vuelta gravel por el valle de Fueva Alta
description: Cycle the difficult 17.9-mile Sobrarbe Geopark BTT Route 5: Aínsa to El Pueyo Loop, exploring geological wonders and Pyrenean villages.
region_intro: Touring cycling routes around Aínsa-Sobrarbe are situated in the heart of the Spanish Pyrenees, at the confluence of the Cinca and Ara rivers. The region features diverse landscapes, including the geological formations of the Sobrarbe Geopark, high plateaus, and varied forest trails. This area offers a range of terrain suitable for touring cyclists, from river valleys to more elevated mountain paths. Best touring cycling routes around Aínsa-Sobrarbe The most popular touring cycling route is Sobrarbe Geopark BTT Route 5: Aínsa to El Pueyo Loop, a 17.9 miles (28.9 km) trail that takes 2 hours 39 minutes to complete. This route explores geological features and Pyrenean villages. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Guaso Exconjuratory – Aínsa Old Town loop from Aínsa, a moderate 14.8 miles (23.8 km) path. This route passes through historical sites and offers views of the medieval town of Aínsa. Local touring cyclists also love the Boltaña Monastery – Puente de los Moscarales loop from Aínsa, a 38.1 miles (61.4 km) trail leading through river valleys and past historical structures, often completed in about 4 hours 13 minutes. Touring cycling around Aínsa-Sobrarbe is defined by the Aragonese Pyrenees, the Sobrarbe Geopark, and the Cinca and Ara river valleys. The network offers options for various ability levels, from moderate paths to more challenging routes. The routes in Aínsa-Sobrarbe are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.2 stars from over 20 reviews. More than 800 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Aínsa-Sobrarbe's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available in Aínsa-Sobrarbe?
A: Aínsa-Sobrarbe offers a wide selection of touring cycling routes, with over 35 options available for you to explore the region's diverse landscapes.

Q: What are the difficulty levels of touring cycling routes in Aínsa-Sobrarbe?
A: The routes in Aínsa-Sobrarbe cater to various skill levels. You'll find options ranging from moderate paths, with 9 routes classified as such, to more challenging rides, with 26 routes rated as difficult. There are also 3 easy routes for a more relaxed experience.

Q: Are there any circular touring cycling routes in the region?
A: Yes, many touring cycling routes in Aínsa-Sobrarbe are designed as loops, allowing you to start and finish in the same location. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e975298774/sobrarbe-geopark-btt-route-5-ainsa-to-el-pueyo-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sobrarbe Geopark BTT Route 5: Aínsa to El Pueyo Loop&lt;/a&gt; is a popular circular option, as is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/10466659" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Guaso Exconjuratory – Aínsa Old Town loop from Aínsa&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What kind of landscapes can I expect to see while touring cycling around Aínsa-Sobrarbe?
A: Aínsa-Sobrarbe, nestled in the Spanish Pyrenees, offers incredibly diverse landscapes. You'll cycle through the geological formations of the Sobrarbe Geopark, along the picturesque Cinca and Ara river valleys, across high plateaus, and through varied forest trails. The region also provides views of the Aragonese Pyrenees and serves as a gateway to stunning natural parks like Ordesa and Monte Perdido.

Q: Are there any historical or cultural attractions along the touring cycling routes?
A: Absolutely. The region is rich in history. Many routes pass by or offer views of the medieval town of Aínsa, a historical-artistic site with its well-preserved streets, castle, and Plaza Mayor. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/10466659" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Guaso Exconjuratory – Aínsa Old Town loop from Aínsa&lt;/a&gt; specifically passes through historical sites and offers views of the old town. You can also visit the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4613486" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Monastery of San Victorián&lt;/a&gt;, accessible via routes that traverse the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4613486" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto de Arro&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What is the best time of year for touring cycling in Aínsa-Sobrarbe?
A: Aínsa-Sobrarbe benefits from a mild climate with abundant sunshine and minimal rainfall, making it suitable for cycling throughout the year. Spring and autumn generally offer the most comfortable temperatures for touring cycling, while summer provides long daylight hours for extended rides.

Q: Where can I find parking near the touring cycling trailheads?
A: Many routes in Aínsa-Sobrarbe, particularly those starting from the town of Aínsa itself, offer convenient parking options. For routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e975298774/sobrarbe-geopark-btt-route-5-ainsa-to-el-pueyo-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sobrarbe Geopark BTT Route 5&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/10466659" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Guaso Exconjuratory – Aínsa Old Town loop&lt;/a&gt;, you can typically find parking within or on the outskirts of Aínsa.

Q: Are there any family-friendly touring cycling routes in Aínsa-Sobrarbe?
A: While many routes in the region are moderate to difficult, there are easier options suitable for families. Look for routes that stick to flatter river valleys or designated paths. The region's extensive network includes some of the 3 easy routes available, which would be more appropriate for a family outing.

Q: What do other cyclists say about touring cycling in Aínsa-Sobrarbe?
A: The touring cycling routes around Aínsa-Sobrarbe are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.2 stars from over 20 reviews. Cyclists often praise the varied terrain, the stunning Pyrenean views, and the opportunity to explore both natural wonders and historical sites.

Q: Can I explore geological features on a touring cycling route?
A: Yes, the Sobrarbe Geopark is a central feature of the region, and several touring cycling routes are designed to showcase its unique geological formations. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e975298774/sobrarbe-geopark-btt-route-5-ainsa-to-el-pueyo-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sobrarbe Geopark BTT Route 5: Aínsa to El Pueyo Loop&lt;/a&gt; is an excellent example, specifically exploring these geological wonders and Pyrenean villages.

Q: Are there options for longer distance touring cycling in the area?
A: Yes, Aínsa-Sobrarbe offers routes suitable for longer distance touring. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/1397602" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Boltaña Monastery – Puente de los Moscarales loop from Aínsa&lt;/a&gt; is a substantial 38.1-mile (61.4 km) trail, typically taking over 4 hours to complete, leading through river valleys and past historical structures.

Q: What natural features can I discover while cycling?
A: Beyond the Geopark, you can discover stunning natural features like the confluence of the Cinca and Ara rivers, and scenic gorges. Routes may pass by highlights such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4446051" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Entremón Gorge&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/961906" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Vero Gorge&lt;/a&gt;. The region's proximity to Ordesa and Monte Perdido National Park also means breathtaking mountain scenery is often in view.

Q: Are there any routes that offer views of the medieval town of Aínsa?
A: Yes, several routes provide excellent views of the historic town of Aínsa. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/10466659" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Guaso Exconjuratory – Aínsa Old Town loop from Aínsa&lt;/a&gt; is specifically noted for offering views of the medieval town, allowing you to appreciate its charm from different perspectives as you cycle.
tips: One of a number of cute hilltop villages around here. This one has a public defibrillator as you get to the top of the climb, just saying 😉

Torrelisa is a small town located to the north of the Sierras de Araguas and Soto, with a vast panorama of the Graus reservoir and the Valle de Fueva-Alta. It is surrounded by the immense rocky massif of Sierra Ferrera.

The Badlands de Soto are a natural space that is located between the town of Aínsa and the Sierra de Soto, where a landscape without vegetation and similar to the distance appears. In recent years it has become famous for the so-called Zona Zero, a set of bicycle routes and for all the types/ages that travel through these spaces.

faqs.question: Is the Sobrarbe Geopark BTT Route 5 suitable for touring bicycles?
faqs.answer: This route is primarily designed as a mountain bike (BTT) trail, meaning it's likely to be challenging for standard touring bicycles. The terrain includes unpaved sections, dirt paths, gravel, and potentially rocky or uneven surfaces. Touring bikes with narrow tires and heavy loads would struggle with traction, comfort, and maneuverability, making it arduous.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on the Aínsa to El Pueyo Loop?
faqs.answer: The terrain is predominantly unpaved, typical for a BTT route. You can expect a mix of dirt paths, gravel sections, and potentially rocky or uneven surfaces. It's an off-road experience, so be prepared for varied ground conditions.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this route?
faqs.answer: The route is approximately 28.8 kilometers (17.9 miles) long and typically takes around 2 hours and 40 minutes to complete by bike. This duration can vary based on your fitness level, the type of bike you're using, and how often you stop to enjoy the scenery.

faqs.question: What natural features and landmarks can I see along the route?
faqs.answer: The route offers breathtaking views of the Pyrenean environment and unique geological formations, as it's set within a UNESCO Global Geopark. You'll pass through charming villages like El Pueyo de Araguás and Torrelisa, and experience the striking landscapes of The Badlands of Soto. Keep an eye out for geological formations like the submarine channel of the Aínsa II turbidite system and outcrops of sandstone and marl, and you might even spot fossilized sea urchins.

faqs.question: Where does the Sobrarbe Geopark BTT Route 5 start, and is there parking?
faqs.answer: The route typically starts and ends in Aínsa, forming a loop. While specific parking details aren't provided, Aínsa is a well-known town, and you should find public parking options available there to access the trailhead.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or entrance fees required for the Sobrarbe Geopark BTT Route 5?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to cycle the Sobrarbe Geopark BTT Route 5 itself. The route is part of the Sobrarbe-Pirineos UNESCO Global Geopark, which is a protected area, but access to the trails is generally free.

faqs.question: When is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The Sobrarbe region experiences a blend of Mediterranean and high-mountain climates. Spring (April-May) and Autumn (September-October) are generally the best times to cycle, offering pleasant temperatures and beautiful scenery. Summers can be hot, and winters may bring snow to higher elevations, making conditions more challenging.

faqs.question: Is the Sobrarbe Geopark BTT Route 5 dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While the Geopark generally welcomes visitors, specific regulations for dogs on BTT routes can vary. It's always best to keep dogs on a leash to protect local wildlife and ensure the safety of other trail users. Be prepared to carry water for your dog, as water sources may not be consistently available.

faqs.question: Does the route intersect with other trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route intersects with several other paths. Notably, it covers 47% of the official "Sobrarbe. BTT. Ruta 5. De Ainsa a El Pueyo" trail and also passes through sections of "ZZ007 - Ruta Bajo Peñas" and "Ainsa - Los Molinos - La Muera - Monasterio de San Victorián - Ermita de la Espelunga".

faqs.question: What makes this trail notable within the Sobrarbe-Pirineos UNESCO Global Geopark?
faqs.answer: This route is one of 13 specially signposted mountain bike routes designed to promote geology within the Geopark. It offers a unique opportunity to combine cycling with geological exploration, allowing you to experience and learn about the Earth's 500-million-year history firsthand through its rock formations and landscapes. It's an ideal trail for those interested in 'geocycling'.
